transcription_cleaned:
Doctor: Good morning. How can I help you today?
Patient: Good morning, doctor. I was referred here from another hospital. I'm 27 weeks pregnant and I had a very scary episode a few weeks ago.
Doctor: I'm glad you came in. Can you walk me through what happened?
Patient: At 22 weeks, I started having vaginal bleeding. I went to the emergency room and they admitted me for bedrest. About two days later, I got terrible pelvic pain and contractions, and I passed a large mass vaginally.
Doctor: That sounds very distressing. Were there any fetal parts in the mass?
Patient: No, the doctors said there were no fetal contents. The baby was fine — they checked the heart tones and everything was okay.
Doctor: Do you know what the pathology showed on that mass?
Patient: Yes, they told me it was a very aggressive-looking tumor under the microscope, with spindle-shaped cells and giant cells. The pathologist said it could be an endometrial stromal sarcoma or an undifferentiated uterine sarcoma, but they weren't sure.
Doctor: I see. And what happened after that — did they do any procedure at the outside hospital?
Patient: Yes, they took me to the operating room and found a hole in the back wall of my vagina that was bleeding. They stitched it closed. My cervix was closed and the baby was still okay.
Doctor: Good. Can you tell me about your obstetric history?
Patient: This is my fourth pregnancy. I've had two therapeutic abortions and one miscarriage.
Doctor: Any prior gynecologic problems — abnormal pap smears, fibroids, infertility?
Patient: No, nothing like that. My periods were always regular, no pain with sex, no unusual bleeding before this pregnancy.
Doctor: Any family history of cancer?
Patient: Yes, actually. Two of my family members had cervical cancer and two had endometrial cancer.
Doctor: That's important to know. We'll order an MRI today to get a better picture of what's happening. Has the fetus had any issues noted on ultrasound?
Patient: Yes, one of the baby's kidneys looks abnormal — they said it's dysplastic.
Doctor: Okay. And how are you feeling now at 27 weeks?
Patient: I feel okay. No more bleeding, no pelvic pain. I'm just worried about what this mass was.
Doctor: Understood. We're going to coordinate with our gynecologic oncology and maternal-fetal medicine teams to make the best plan for you and your baby.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. I just want to make sure we're both safe.
clinical_summary: A 31-year-old G4P0 woman at 27 weeks gestation presented with a history of vaginal bleeding and expulsion of a large mass at 22 weeks, diagnosed as a high-grade sarcoma. She has a family history of cervical and endometrial cancer, and the fetus has a dysplastic kidney. A multidisciplinary team planned a cesarean hysterectomy at 36 weeks, and postpartum management included treatment for recurrent sarcoma. She remains disease-free 58 months after brachytherapy.

differential_diagnosis.rationale: The patient was diagnosed with a high-grade sarcoma postpartum, which aligns with the initial presentation of a large pelvic mass and her family history of cancer.
Given the patient's family history of endometrial cancer and the ER/PR positive status of the tumor, an endometrial stromal sarcoma is a plausible diagnosis.
Leiomyosarcoma is a type of uterine sarcoma that could present with a pelvic mass and is consistent with the patient's age and the hormonal receptor status of the tumor.

final_report.subjective: The patient is a 31-year-old woman who is currently 27 weeks pregnant. She reports a significant episode at 22 weeks of gestation, where she experienced vaginal bleeding and subsequently passed a large mass vaginally. She was admitted to the hospital for bedrest and later developed pelvic pain and contractions. The mass was analyzed and suspected to be a high-grade sarcoma. The patient has a family history of cervical and endometrial cancer. She has had two therapeutic abortions and one miscarriage in the past. She is concerned about the implications of the mass on her pregnancy and her health.
final_report.objective: On examination, the patient is 27 weeks pregnant with no current vaginal bleeding or pelvic pain. Her obstetric history includes a fetal anomaly, specifically a dysplastic kidney noted on ultrasound. A pelvic examination revealed no residual vaginal mass, and a recent Pap smear was normal with negative HPV results. An MRI of the pelvis, abdomen, and chest showed no metastatic disease but confirmed the presence of a Nabothian cyst in the cervix and the fetal renal anomaly. The patient is stable, and fetal heart tones are normal. There is a plan for a cesarean hysterectomy at 36 weeks gestation.
final_report.assessment: The patient was diagnosed with a high-grade vaginal sarcoma, which was initially suspected after the expulsion of a large mass at 22 weeks of gestation. The differential diagnosis includes endometrial stromal sarcoma and leiomyosarcoma, given the tumor's ER/PR positive status and the patient's family history of gynecological cancers. The presence of a fetal renal anomaly adds complexity to the case, but there is no evidence of metastatic disease at this time. The patient's condition requires careful monitoring and coordination with oncology and maternal-fetal medicine specialists.
final_report.plan: The plan involves a multidisciplinary approach to manage the patient's condition and ensure the safety of both the mother and the fetus. A cesarean hysterectomy is scheduled for 36 weeks gestation to address the sarcoma while minimizing risks to the fetus. Betamethasone will be administered to promote fetal lung maturity prior to delivery. Postpartum, the patient will undergo further evaluation and treatment for the sarcoma, including possible brachytherapy. Regular follow-up appointments will be necessary to monitor for any recurrence of the sarcoma and to manage the fetal renal anomaly. The patient will be supported by a team of specialists throughout her treatment and recovery.
